data.title: As a DSO user, I get faster ticket list, show, and transition commands because each invokes at most one python3 process instead of 2-3 chained subprocesses
data.description:
## Description

**What**: Move `to_llm` from `ticket-llm-format.py` (standalone script loaded via importlib) into the `ticket_reducer` Python package as `ticket_reducer/llm_format.py`. Migrate `ticket-list.sh`, `ticket-show.sh`, and `ticket-transition.sh` from their current 2–3-subprocess pipelines (reducer → filter → llm-format) into single-process Python entries that import the package. Update the test file that imports `to_llm` directly. Preserve the existing `fcntl.flock` critical section boundary in `ticket-transition.sh` — the single-process consolidation must NOT widen the lock scope.

**Why**: Each of the three commands today spawns 2–3 separate `python3` subprocesses. At ~30ms startup cost per subprocess, that's 60–100ms of avoidable latency per invocation. Across a sprint session (dozens of ticket commands), this compounds to several seconds of dead time. Consolidating to a single import-based entry eliminates the overhead.

**Scope**:
- IN: Move `to_llm` to `ticket_reducer/llm_format.py`; update all callers of `to_llm` — `ticket-list.sh`, `ticket-show.sh`, `ticket-transition.sh`, and any test file importing `to_llm` directly (confirmed by pre-story grep including `.md` files); each of the three scripts invokes at most one `python3` process for its reducer+format pipeline; preserve `fcntl.flock` boundary in `ticket-transition.sh`
- OUT: Consolidation of cheap `python3 -c "import json..."` one-liners in `ticket-create.sh`, `ticket-edit.sh`, etc. (applied YAGNI — they're already <30ms and not meaningfully optimizable); deletion of `ticket-llm-format.py` (can be left as a compatibility shim or removed depending on grep audit results)

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, `to_llm` is importable as `from ticket_reducer.llm_format import to_llm`, the old `ticket-llm-format.py` importlib dance in `ticket-list.sh` and `ticket-show.sh` is replaced with a normal import, and all test callers are updated
  ← Satisfies: "`to_llm` is moved into the `ticket_reducer` package as `ticket_reducer/llm_format.py`. All callers are updated..."
- When this story is complete, `ticket-list.sh`, `ticket-show.sh`, and `ticket-transition.sh` each invoke at most one `python3` process for their reducer+format pipeline, verified by a test that counts subprocess invocations per command
  ← Satisfies: "Each of the three scripts invokes at most one `python3` process for its reducer+format pipeline. Verified by a test that counts subprocess invocations per command"
- When this story is complete, the `fcntl.flock` critical section boundary in `ticket-transition.sh` is preserved (not widened), verified by a test that asserts the lock is not held across work that is currently outside it
  ← Satisfies: "The existing `fcntl.flock` critical section boundary in `ticket-transition.sh` is preserved (not widened)"

## Considerations

- [Reliability] Pre-story grep gate MUST include `.sh`, `.py`, AND `.md` files — SKILL and documentation refs to `to_llm` module path must be caught before the move. Commit the grep output to the story notes so the audit is reviewable.
- [Testing] Subprocess-count assertion: count via wrapper script that intercepts python3 invocations (e.g., a PATH-shadowed `python3` that logs each exec), OR via structural assertion on the shell script text (regex for `python3` occurrences). Structural is simpler and sufficient for regression detection.
- [Reliability] Flock boundary test: assert the lock is released BEFORE any non-critical-section work (e.g., formatting) happens in the consolidated process. Easy test: inject a `DSO_TEST_ASSERT_LOCK_RELEASED=1` env var that makes the format step call a helper that checks lock state.
- [Maintainability] Merge conflict risk with S1a/S1b — epic mandates merge order S1 → S2. Coordinate merge timing.
- [Security] No new auth/trust boundaries — N/A
- [Accessibility] No UI changes — N/A

## Escalation Policy

**Escalation policy**: Proceed unless a significant assumption is required to continue — one that could send the implementation in the wrong direction. Escalate only when genuinely blocked without a reasonable inference. Document all assumptions made without escalating.
